questions.text: I hold the view that my time is best invested in service to the people around me.
I frequently take on captain or leadership roles in team settings.
I give myself permission to change course whenever circumstances shift.
I sense when someone is being polite out of obligation rather than genuine warmth.
I am easily swept up in the emotional energy of a crowd or a room.
I feel that the moral concern generated by witnessing suffering is the force that keeps my ethical commitments grounded in reality.
I prepare for meetings by reviewing agendas and relevant materials in advance.
I notice that I can shift my attention away from emotional stimuli relatively easily.
I pick up on subtle changes in how someone addresses me that signal a shift in our relationship.
I tend to redirect conversations toward topics that relate to my own goals.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel when confronted with suffering is the experience that gives my sense of justice its emotional grounding and its motivational force.
I feel that accumulating things for myself is less important than ensuring others are provided for.
I keep my workspace meticulously organized because it helps me think clearly.
I wait to hear everyone's perspective before forming a group plan.
I believe that professionals who maintain their analytical composure during tragic events are providing a service that emotional responders cannot.
I feel that my emotions operate on a hair trigger — it doesn't take much to set them off.
I tend to miss the warning signs that a conversation is heading toward conflict.
I often champion a particular direction and rally others behind it.
